responses: I am an attorney, former Drug Court Judge, and community leader who has dedicated my career to public service, the rule of law, and strengthening Wyoming communities. I earned an MBA and law degree and have spent years helping individuals, families, businesses, and local governments navigate complex legal issues. I currently serve as President of the Cody Country Chamber of Commerce and have been recognized for professional excellence by various groups including being one of the Wyoming Chamber of Commerce's 40 Under 40. My background in law, business, and community service gives me the experience and practical perspective needed to represent House District 50 and fight for limited government, local control, and Wyoming values.

Wyoming faces three major challenges: rising costs for families and businesses, economic uncertainty tied to the energy industry, and a growing disconnect between government and local communities. At the same time, Wyoming has tremendous opportunities to strengthen its economy, develop new energy technologies, support small businesses, and attract families who value freedom and our way of life.

As a legislator, I would focus on making Wyoming more affordable, protecting Wyoming's energy industries while encouraging innovation, and returning more decision-making authority to local communities. I support responsible government spending, reducing unnecessary regulations, and preserving the constitutional rights and freedoms that make Wyoming unique. My goal is to keep Wyoming affordable, prosperous, and independent for future generations.

Healthcare in Wyoming is expensive for many of the same reasons it is expensive across the country: a lack of competition, excessive bureaucracy, and policies that drive up costs without improving care. The Legislature should focus on increasing access to providers, reducing unnecessary regulations, expanding telehealth options, and improving price transparency so patients can make informed decisions.

Wyoming should encourage market-based solutions that increase competition and lower costs while ensuring rural communities maintain access to essential healthcare services. I support efforts that give patients more control over their healthcare decisions and dollars, while avoiding one-size-fits-all approaches that increase costs or create greater dependence on government programs.

I do not support the sale or large-scale transfer of Wyoming’s public lands. Public lands are an important part of our state’s heritage, economy, and way of life. They support recreation, hunting, grazing, tourism, and multiple-use access that benefits Wyoming residents and visitors alike.

I do support stronger local input and better management of state and federal lands. Decisions affecting Wyoming should involve the people who live, work, and recreate here, not just distant bureaucracies. I will advocate for policies that promote responsible multiple-use management, protect public access, support responsible resource development, and ensure these lands continue to benefit future generations of Wyomingites.

Wyoming taxpayers deserve meaningful property tax relief, but we must also recognize that no two counties are the same. Counties that experienced dramatic increases in property valuations should not be treated the same as counties that saw little change. A one-size-fits-all reduction risks creating winners and losers across the state.

I support returning residential property valuations to 2019 levels and establishing a reasonable cap on future increases from that point forward. This approach would provide substantial relief in areas where valuations surged while allowing counties that were less affected to maintain a stable tax base and continue funding essential local services. It offers a balanced solution that restores predictability and affordability for homeowners while respecting local needs and preserving local control.

The Legislature has an important role in creating an environment where businesses, workers, and communities can succeed, but it should not try to pick winners and losers in the marketplace. The state's primary responsibility is to provide a stable regulatory environment, maintain essential infrastructure, support workforce development, and keep taxes competitive so businesses have the confidence to invest in Wyoming.

As President of the Cody Country Chamber of Commerce, I have seen firsthand how local businesses drive economic growth and strengthen communities. Economic development is most successful when it is driven from the ground up by entrepreneurs, local leaders, and private investment—not directed from Cheyenne. I support policies that reduce barriers to growth, encourage innovation, and allow Wyoming businesses and workers to thrive while preserving the values and quality of life that make our state unique.

Campaign contributions should be transparent so voters know who is supporting candidates and political causes. Wyoming's elections should be decided by Wyoming voters, not by outside organizations or special interests seeking to influence our state's future.

I am particularly concerned about the growing role of out-of-state money in Wyoming elections. Regardless of political ideology, Wyoming residents should be able to see who is funding campaigns and independent expenditures and make informed decisions accordingly. I support strong transparency and disclosure requirements so voters have access to timely and accurate information. While Wyoming's current system generally provides meaningful disclosure, I am open to reforms that increase transparency and help ensure Wyoming elections remain accountable to the people of Wyoming.

I am an attorney, former Drug Court Judge, and community leader who has dedicated my career to public service, the rule of law, and strengthening Wyoming communities. I earned an MBA and law degree and have spent years helping individuals, families, businesses, and local governments navigate complex legal issues. questions: Where do you live currently? How long have you lived there? How long have you lived in Wyoming? Where were you born?
What age will you be on Election Day, Nov. 3, 2026?
Please tell the voters about yourself including your background and qualifications.
What are the biggest challenges and opportunities facing Wyoming today? As a legislator, how would you address them?

We asked WyoFile readers to rank issues that are important to them, and healthcare costs and access topped the list. What can the Legislature do to make healthcare more affordable and accessible to Wyomingites?
Do you support efforts to sell or transfer public land in Wyoming? How do you plan to ensure that state and federal land is managed in the best interest of the people of Wyoming?
No two Wyoming counties are the same when it comes to how they generate revenue. As a legislator, how would you approach the public's desire for property tax relief with local revenue needs?
What role, if any, should the Wyoming Legislature play in economic development?
Explain your values when it comes to campaign donations. Does Wyoming's system for monitoring campaign donations and expenditures need reform?
pageProps.questionnaireIntro:
The answers below were solicited from candidates via a written questionnaire created by WyoFile reporters and editors in June 2026. Responses are presented exactly as submitted, without fact-checking, wordsmithing or editing for grammar, punctuation or spelling errors. 

Legislative candidates were invited to respond to the questionnaire several times by email and by phone. Out of fairness to the candidates who met the deadline, WyoFile will not add responses after the guide's publication.
pageProps.aboutProject:
WyoFile produced this guide with coding and web development by Thomas Musselman, editing by Tennessee Watson and production assistance by Calla Shosh. Contact Tennessee Watson with questions, corrections or suggestions at <tennessee@wyofile.com>.

WyoFile is a Wyoming 501(c)3 nonprofit, independent, member-supported, public-interest news service reporting on the people, places and policy of Wyoming. WyoFile's [donor base](https://wyofile.com/supporters/) is politically diverse, including some donors who are candidates in this year's election. We maintain separation between sources of revenue and editorial direction, decisions and oversight. Acceptance of financial support does not constitute implied or actual endorsement of donors or their products, services or opinions, and does not confer editorial influence, nor does donor support imply any endorsement of WyoFile's reporting, commentary or editorial position.

This material is available for republication by select media outlets. Contact Collaborations Editor Rebecca Huntington at <rebecca@wyofile.com> to learn how.
